votes.reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code and reproducing the behavior.

Code (src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:146-156): on the main thread, _install_flush_on_exit captures `prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` and installs a handler that flushes/shuts down the tracer, then only re-raises to the prior handler via `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. It replaces SIGTERM's disposition unconditionally.

Empirical facts:
1. `signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` returns `signal.SIG_DFL` (value 0) when no Python handler is installed (the common production case), and `callable(SIG_DFL)` is False. So the `if callable(prior)` branch is skipped and the handler returns normally — termination is swallowed.
2. Reproduced with the exact handler logic: a process that installs the handler then sends itself SIGTERM prints "STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM (termination swallowed)" and exits 0.
3. Control: a process with no handler installed that sends itself SIGTERM dies with exit 143 (128+SIGTERM). This proves the installed handler genuinely changes behavior — the process that would have died now survives until SIGKILL escalation (e.g., docker stop's 10s grace, k8s terminationGracePeriod).

Reachability is real and on by default:
- Tracing is enabled unless ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING is set (_disabled, line 53-54), so the default path runs.
- bootstrap_tracing() -> _install_flush_on_exit() is called in the synchronous run path (pipeline.py:381) and resume/orchestration path (orchestration/graph.py:111,139). These execute on the caller's thread; for the CLI/run-study entry there is no thread/process pool spawning, so it is the main thread, satisfying the `threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread()` guard at tracing.py:146.

Test gap confirmed: tests/test_observability.py:195-231 monkeypatches getsignal to return SIG_DFL (line 211), invokes the handler (line 228), and only asserts flush/shutdown ran (calls["shutdown"] >= 1). It never asserts the process would still terminate, so the test passes despite the swallowed-termination defect.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect (graceful-shutdown / container-stop semantics broken in the default configuration), not covered by any out-of-scope-by-design exclusion. A correct fix would re-raise the default disposition explicitly when prior is SIG_DFL (e.g., reset to SIG_DFL and re-deliver, or raise SystemExit / os.kill with default) and add a test asserting termination still occurs.

Verified by reading the code and reproducing the behavior.

CODE: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:136-158 — _install_flush_on_exit installs a SIGTERM handler that flushes/shuts down the tracer, then only calls the prior handler `if callable(prior)`. There is no else branch.

CLAIM 1 (SIG_DFL not callable): Confirmed empirically — `callable(signal.SIG_DFL)` returns False (SIG_DFL == 0). A fresh process has SIGTERM disposition == SIG_DFL.

CLAIM 2 (handler swallows termination): Reproduced. A script mimicking the handler exactly (prior=getsignal=SIG_DFL, install _handler, os.kill(self, SIGTERM)) printed "STILL ALIVE after SIGTERM" and exited 0. Contrast: an identical script WITHOUT the handler exited 143 (128+SIGTERM). So the installed handler converts the default terminate-on-SIGTERM into a no-op, meaning docker stop/kill/k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation.

CLAIM 3 (tracing on by default): Confirmed. _disabled() (line 53-54) is pure opt-out via ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING; default is enabled.

CLAIM 4 (bootstrap on main thread in run/resume): Confirmed. orchestration/graph.py run() (110-111) and resume() (138-139) both call bootstrap_tracing() synchronously with trace=True default. bootstrap_tracing -> _install_flush_on_exit(provider) (tracing.py:97); the Phoenix register() provider exposes force_flush, so the SIGTERM handler is installed when running on the main thread.

CLAIM 5 (test never asserts termination): Confirmed. tests/test_observability.py:195-229 (test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm) monkeypatches signal.getsignal to return SIG_DFL (line 211) — the exact vulnerable case — then invokes the handler and only asserts calls["shutdown"] >= 1 (line 229). It never asserts the process would still terminate, so the defect is not caught.

This is a real production-readiness defect (graceful shutdown broken under the most common production case of no pre-existing Python SIGTERM handler) affecting every run/resume invocation. It is not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion. Suggested fix: when prior is SIG_DFL (or SIG_IGN), restore default disposition and re-raise/re-deliver the signal (e.g., signal.signal(SIGTERM, SIG_DFL); os.kill(os.getpid(), SIGTERM)) or raise SystemExit(143) after flushing.

Reproduced the defect end-to-end with the exact CI-pinned tooling and command.

What I read:
- .github/workflows/ci.yml ~126-146 (audit job "Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject"): installs `uv==0.11.21`, then runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock` and `diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/...regen.lock) || { echo ...; exit 1; }` (same for requirements-core.lock and requirements-build.lock). The `diff || exit 1` makes any mismatch a hard CI failure.
- scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh: same uv pin + same compile command, no resolution pin.
- pyproject.toml: dependencies use open ranges (e.g. `mlflow>=3.1,<4`, `arize-phoenix>=17,<18`, etc.) and ~218 transitive deps are unconstrained.

What I ran (exactly mirroring CI):
1. Installed `uv==0.11.21` (verified: `uv 0.11.21 (5aa65dd7a 2026-06-11)`).
2. Ran the gate's exact compile command against live PyPI -> EXIT 0 (resolution succeeded; no offline/error artifact).
3. Ran the gate's exact header-stripped diff vs the committed requirements.lock -> diff EXIT 1 (mismatch), 48 differing lines.

What I observed:
- Committed requirements.lock pins `mlflow==3.13.0`; the fresh re-resolution produced `mlflow==3.14.0` — precisely the example the finding cited (3.13.0->3.14.0).
- Additional in-range drift: arize-phoenix 17.6.0->17.7.0, mlflow-skinny/mlflow-tracing 3.13.0->3.14.0, certifi 2026.5.20->2026.6.17, tqdm 4.68.2->4.68.3.
- None of this drift is attributable to any code/dep change in a PR — it is purely upstream packages publishing new in-range releases after the lockfile was committed. The diff -> `exit 1` means the audit job goes red on a clean checkout today, blocking merges.

Mitigation check: grepped .github/, pyproject.toml, scripts/, and looked for uv.toml — there is NO `--exclude-newer`, no resolution-date pin, no UV_EXCLUDE_NEWER, no uv.toml. The `uv==0.11.21` pin only stabilizes output format/ordering (its stated purpose, #F-031); it does NOT pin the resolution target, so uv still selects the newest in-range versions at CI wall-clock time. The reproducibility guard is itself non-reproducible.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect: a clean-checkout PR that touches nothing dependency-related will fail the audit gate the moment any in-range transitive release lands, and it is currently failing (mlflow 3.14.0). Not out-of-scope by design. The fix is to add `--exclude-newer <committed-date>` (or an equivalent resolution-date pin / uv.toml setting) to both the CI gate and regenerate_lockfiles.sh so the sync check resolves against the same point-in-time index the lockfile was built from.

Confirmed by direct reproduction with the CI-pinned uv==0.11.21.

Code read: .github/workflows/ci.yml ~126-146 (audit job, step "Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)") runs `uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock` and diffs (grep -v '^#') against the committed requirements.lock, doing `exit 1` on any diff. The same pattern repeats for requirements-core.lock and requirements-build.lock. scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh uses the identical compile commands.

No resolution-date pin anywhere: `grep` for exclude-newer/exclude_newer/resolution found NO `--exclude-newer` flag in ci.yml or the regen script; there is no uv.toml and no [tool.uv] table in pyproject.toml. Dependencies are declared with floored-but-open ranges (e.g. `mlflow>=3.1,<4`, `arize-phoenix>=17,<18`, `tqdm` transitive), so uv resolves the NEWEST in-range version at wall-clock time.

Reproduction (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay; source .venv/bin/activate; uv 0.11.21 confirmed): re-ran the exact CI compile command, then the exact CI diff. diff exit=1 (gate would fail). Version lines that drifted purely from newer PyPI publishes (pyproject.toml unchanged):
  mlflow 3.13.0 -> 3.14.0  (the finding's exact example)
  mlflow-skinny / mlflow-tracing 3.13.0 -> 3.14.0
  arize-phoenix 17.6.0 -> 17.7.0
  certifi 2026.5.20 -> 2026.6.17
  tqdm 4.68.2 -> 4.68.3
48-line diff total.

This is a genuine production-readiness/CI-correctness defect: the "reproducibility guard" is itself irreproducible. On a clean checkout, with no dependency-related change, the audit job's first substantive step goes red and blocks merges the instant any of ~218 transitive packages ships an in-range release. It is currently failing exactly as claimed. Not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion. The fix is to pin resolution time (e.g. `--exclude-newer <date>` in both the CI compile and regenerate_lockfiles.sh, or an equivalent [tool.uv] exclude-newer).

All sub-claims of K-OBS-2 reproduce empirically and against the actual source.

(1) freeze() only rejects UNHASHABLE values. src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:119-126 does `try: hash(value) except TypeError: raise`. A datetime, bytes, or Enum is hashable, so it passes through unchanged, and unfreeze() (line 164) returns leaves unchanged. Verified: `freeze({"evidence":{"when":datetime,"raw":b"..."}})` succeeds and unfreeze yields back datetime/bytes leaves.

(2) entries_to_records + json.dump raises TypeError on such a payload. Verified: building a trail with `trail.record("gate", ..., evidence={"when": datetime})` then json.dumps(entries_to_records(...)) raises `TypeError: Object of type datetime is not JSON serializable`.

(3) Temp-file leak. In _log_trail (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:740-754) the NamedTemporaryFile uses delete=False; `path = fh.name` is line 744, reached ONLY after json.dump on line 743 succeeds. When json.dump raises, `path` is never bound, the `with` block exits WITHOUT deleting the file (delete=False), and the try/finally cleanup at 748-754 is never reached because the exception propagates first. Verified by replicating the exact body: a `*_provenance.json` temp file with partial provenance contents was left on disk after the TypeError. (I also observed ~85 pre-existing leaked *_provenance.json files in tmp, consistent with this happening in practice.)

(4) Silent drop, no warning. track() (pipeline.py:317-324) catches Exception and records only a generic `tracking_error` provenance entry with NO `_log.warning` (confirmed: track() body contains 'tracking_error' but not '_log.warning'; contrast the start_run failure path at line 365 which DOES warn). So the provenance artifact never attaches to the MLflow run and nothing is logged to operators.

Scope: this is not contrived input. ProvenanceTrail.as_recorder (provenance.py:169-186) records arbitrary `evidence = dict(getattr(decision,'evidence',{}))` from operator/handler-supplied GateDecision objects; a datetime/bytes/Enum in gate evidence is a realistic value. The defect silently breaks the stated #H-013 (no temp-file leak) and #G-009 (provenance attaches) guarantees. The minor wording nit in the finding (it says path is assigned "AFTER" the dump — it is, but on the immediately following line inside the same with-block) does not change the outcome: path is unassigned at the moment the exception propagates, so the finally cleanup is genuinely unreachable. Genuine, in-scope production defect.

Verified by reading the code and reproducing the failure end-to-end.

Mechanism (confirmed in source):
- _frozen.freeze (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:82-126) only rejects UNHASHABLE leaves; a hashable-but-non-JSON value (bytes, datetime, Decimal, UUID, Enum) passes through unchanged.
- ProvenanceTrail._record_locked (provenance.py:144) digests via _digest -> canonical_json, and _canonical.canonical_plain (lines 41-58) explicitly CANONICALIZES bytes (__bytes__), datetime/date/time (__temporal__), Decimal, UUID. So such a payload does NOT raise at record() time — the entry is stored and the hash chain builds and even verify()s clean.
- entries_to_records (provenance.py:226-240) calls unfreeze on the payload; unfreeze (_frozen.py:142-164) returns these leaves unchanged (they are not Mapping/frozenset/list/tuple). The non-JSON leaf survives into the plain record.
- _log_trail (pipeline.py:740-754): json.dump(list(entries_to_records(...)), fh) executes BEFORE `path = fh.name`. bare json.dump has no default= handler, so it raises TypeError on the leaf. Because the exception fires before line 744, `path` is never bound and the try/finally os.unlink cleanup is unreachable. The NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) is orphaned on disk WITH provenance contents (#H-013 violated).
- track (pipeline.py:317-324) catches Exception and records only a generic "tracking_error" entry, with NO _log.warning — the trail artifact silently never attaches to the run (#G-009 violated, silently).

Reproductions (run under .venv): 
1) record() with evidence={"raw": b"..."} succeeds; replicating _log_trail's body, json.dump raised "Object of type bytes is not JSON serializable", `path` stayed unassigned, and a 144-byte temp file with provenance JSON was left on disk (leak confirmed).
2) The gate path: as_recorder records a 'gate' entry carrying a datetime in evidence; trail.verify() passes (chain intact) yet json.dumps fails on the datetime.
3) Most realistic production path: trail.record("baseline", ..., determinism={"built_at": datetime(...), "tol": Decimal("0.01")}) — exactly the pipeline.py:484-490 baseline record, whose contents/determinism come from the caller-supplied (opaque, dataset-specific) baseline_builder. Chain verifies; json.dump crashes.

Both referenced guarantees are real and documented (pipeline.py:728 #G-009, :747 #H-013; tests/test_pipeline.py:451/455; docs/hardening/PASS-4.md, PASS-5.md).

Scope check: The "opaque inputs / dataset-agnostic engine" exclusion does NOT cover this. The defect is an internal inconsistency in the engine itself: it deliberately accepts these leaves into the chain (lenient freeze + canonical_json type allowlist) but then serializes them with a bare json.dump lacking a default= coercer (it could route through unfreeze->canonical_plain-style tagging, as canonical_json already does for hashing). Caller data flows into engine-owned baseline/determinism/feature_names provenance records (pipeline.py:464-490) via opaque-by-design seams, so the trigger is reachable through legitimate use, not caller misuse. The failure is silent (no warning), drops the audit artifact, and leaks a temp file — a genuine production-readiness defect. Note one minor inaccuracy in the finding: pipeline.run_gate (line 331-338) does NOT forward gate evidence to the trail, so the precise "gate evidence" example is not the live injection point; but the baseline determinism/contents and feature_names records are, so the finding's substance holds.

Key files: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py (~733-756 _log_trail, ~317-324 track, 484-490 baseline record), src/assay_engine/provenance.py (144 _digest-before-freeze, 226-240 entries_to_records), src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (82-126 freeze, 142-164 unfreeze), src/assay_engine/_canonical.py (32-65 canonical_plain).

Verified empirically against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py (require_local_uri, is_loopback_host) and confirmed reachability via src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:162-163 (get_postgres_connection_string routes the operator-supplied ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL through require_local_uri).

Reproduction (in .venv):
- DSN form `host=/var/run/postgresql dbname=mydb` -> REJECTED with NonLocalEndpointError ("DSN names non-loopback host"). The DSN branch (_local.py:144-153) matches the `host` token and tests is_loopback_host("/var/run/postgresql"), which returns False.
- URI form `postgresql:///mydb?host=/var/run/postgresql` -> REJECTED. It has an empty authority (no netloc), so it falls to the DSN branch via the `=` in the query string; the `host=/var/run/postgresql` token is matched and rejected the same way.
- is_loopback_host('/var/run/postgresql') == False (confirmed): a leading-"/" socket directory is neither "localhost" nor a parseable IP, so it fails the loopback test.

This is the libpq-standard form for a Unix-domain socket — a connection that never touches the network and is strictly more data-sovereign than TCP loopback. Yet:
- TCP loopback IS accepted (`postgresql://localhost/db`, `host=localhost`).
- A bare local path IS accepted: require_local_uri('/var/run/postgresql') returns the path unchanged (no `=`, single leading slash, so it hits the bare-path branch at :165). So the same string is accepted as a path but rejected as a libpq host token — an inconsistency that blocks the most local connection method.

Fail-closed behavior remains correct: `//server/share` -> REJECTED (authority branch), and `\\server\share` -> REJECTED (UNC branch at :160-164). So a fix to allow leading-"/" socket hosts must be scoped narrowly (only host/hostaddr tokens beginning with "/", not the `//`/UNC authority forms) to avoid weakening the UNC guard.

No test or doc declares the rejection intentional: grep of tests/, docs/, and ADR-0003 found no mention of Unix sockets; tests/test_persistence.py covers loopback enforcement, query-host override, DSN quoting/whitespace, multi-host, and UNC fail-closed, but never the legitimate socket form. ADR-0003 contains no socket/path carve-out either.

Not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion: on-box data sovereignty is the guard's own stated purpose (ADR-0003), and a Unix socket is the most on-box option; rejecting it is a genuine defect (a usability/correctness gap that forces operators away from the most sovereign connection path), not a deliberate design choice. Medium severity is appropriate: it does not weaken security (it is over-strict, failing safe) but it breaks a standard, recommended local Postgres configuration.

Reproduced empirically and confirmed the claim. In /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py, require_local_uri() treats any host/hostaddr value through is_loopback_host(), which only accepts the literal "localhost" or an IP that parses as loopback. A libpq Unix-domain-socket directory path (leading "/", e.g. "/var/run/postgresql") is neither, so it is rejected.

Observed (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...):
- require_local_uri("postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql", ...) -> REJECT (NonLocalEndpointError, "DSN/URI query host points off-box"). Empty netloc routes it into the "=" DSN branch; the host=/var/run/postgresql token's value fails is_loopback_host.
- require_local_uri("host=/var/run/postgresql dbname=db", ...) -> REJECT (DSN branch, same cause).
- require_local_uri("postgresql://localhost:5432/db", ...) -> ACCEPT (TCP loopback passes).
- is_loopback_host("/var/run/postgresql") -> False.
- Fail-closed for remote still holds: "//server/share" -> REJECT (authority branch), and "\\\\server\\share" (UNC) -> REJECT (UNC-style path branch). So tightening to allow Unix sockets would not weaken UNC handling.

This is a genuine defect: a Unix-domain socket never touches the network and is strictly more data-sovereign than TCP loopback (which IS accepted), yet the guard refuses the one local form that cannot leave the box. It is reachable in production: checkpoint.py:162-163 routes the operator-supplied ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL env var through require_local_uri (get_postgres_connection_string), and tracking.py:30 does the same for the tracking URI. An operator following standard Postgres practice (peer-auth over the postgresql socket) would be blocked, with no fallback.

Not out-of-scope-by-design and not intentional: I grepped src/, tests/, and docs/. No test or doc declares Unix-socket rejection intentional. tests/test_persistence.py only exercises TCP hostnames (db.evil.com, 127.0.0.1, localhost, IP-literal hostaddr) and explicitly accepts loopback (test_connection_string_accepts_local_dsn_with_spaces with host=127.0.0.1). The module docstring's stated intent is "non-local address fails loud rather than silently shipping data off the machine" — a Unix socket ships nothing off the machine, so rejecting it contradicts the guard's own purpose rather than implementing it. The fix is contained (recognize a leading-"/" host as a local socket path in is_loopback_host's caller or require_local_uri), with no impact on the remote/UNC fail-closed behavior verified above.

Verified all claims by reading scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh, .github/workflows/ci.yml, .github/dependabot.yml, and requirements-build.lock.

CI gates three lockfiles in the sync step: requirements.lock (ci.yml:132-135), requirements-core.lock (ci.yml:138-140), and requirements-build.lock (ci.yml:143-146). The build-lock check generates a fresh lock via `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' | uv pip compile ... --generate-hashes` and `diff`s it, failing with `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale` on any drift.

The documented remediation script regenerates only requirements.lock (line 21) and requirements-core.lock (line 22). `grep -c requirements-build.lock scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` returns 0, confirming omission; its closing echo (line 24) explicitly says only "requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock". 

.github/dependabot.yml:9 names exactly this script as the way to make a Dependabot PR mergeable, so a maintainer following the documented workflow regenerates 2 of 3 CI-gated locks and stays red, with no scripted path for the third (must hand-reconstruct the CI command from the error text).

The churn risk is real: requirements-build.lock:23 pins `trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19`, a date-versioned, frequently-released transitive of hatchling that will drift and trip the gate.

This is a genuine, in-scope operational/maintainability defect (broken documented remediation workflow), not one of the out-of-scope exclusions, and is concretely fixable by adding the build-lock compile to the script and updating its echo message.

Reproduced empirically against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

(1) scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh regenerates ONLY requirements.lock (line 21) and requirements-core.lock (line 22); its closing echo (line 24) says "regenerated requirements.lock + requirements-core.lock". `grep -c requirements-build.lock scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` returns 0 — the build lock is genuinely omitted.

(2) CI does gate requirements-build.lock for staleness. .github/workflows/ci.yml lines 143-146 build `printf 'hatchling\neditables\n'`, run `uv pip compile ... -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock`, diff against the committed requirements-build.lock, and on mismatch emit `::error::requirements-build.lock is stale` and `exit 1`. So a stale build lock fails CI red.

(3) Both documented remediation pointers steer maintainers to the script that does NOT fix the build lock: .github/dependabot.yml line 9 ("On a Dependabot PR, run `scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh` and commit the result to make it mergeable") and the script header (lines 1-11) frame it as THE lockfile-regeneration workflow. A maintainer following the documented path regenerates two of three locks and stays red, with no scripted command for the build lock — they must hand-reconstruct the `uv pip compile <(printf ...) ...` invocation from the CI error text.

(4) The drift risk is real, not theoretical: requirements-build.lock pins trove-classifiers==2026.6.1.19 (a frequently, date-stamped released package, transitively via hatchling) plus hatchling==1.30.1 and editables==0.6, all of which receive upstream updates that will make the lock stale over time.

This is a genuine operational/reproducibility defect: a documented remediation workflow that leaves a CI-gated artifact unfixed, blocking otherwise-valid (e.g. Dependabot) PRs. Not covered by any out-of-scope exclusion. Low-cost fix: add the build-lock `uv pip compile` line to regenerate_lockfiles.sh and update its echo. Severity medium is appropriate (operability/CI-friction, not security).

Verified by direct reading and empirical reproduction.

WHAT I READ:
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml line 105-106: the integration job's test step is exactly `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` — no `--cov-fail-under` flag.
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml: there is no `[tool.coverage]` / `[tool.coverage.report]` section and no `fail_under`. The only coverage-related entry is line 41: `"pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)`.
- No `.coveragerc`, `setup.cfg`, or `tox.ini` exists (ls confirmed absent), and a repo-wide grep for `fail_under|fail-under|cov-fail` returned zero matches. So no coverage threshold is configured anywhere.

EMPIRICAL REPRODUCTION (using /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python, pytest-cov 7.1.0):
Built a module with genuinely uncovered multi-line function bodies (45% coverage) and a passing test, then ran the exact CI flags vs. a thresholded run:
- `pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-report=term-missing` -> TOTAL 45% coverage, "1 passed", EXIT=0 (green).
- `pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-fail-under=80` -> "Coverage failure: total of 45 is less than fail-under=80", EXIT=1 (red).

This proves the claim's core mechanism: `pytest --cov` without a threshold exits 0 regardless of how low coverage is, so deleting tests or adding a large untested module would still pass CI green. Coverage is measured (printed to the terminal report) but never enforced — a vacuous gate, and the pyproject comment framing pytest-cov as added to "gate coverage (#103)" overstates what is actually enforced.

NOT out-of-scope: this is a CI/ops enforcement gap in the project's own workflow (not the dataset-agnostic engine, optional backends, transcripts, data-sovereignty, or a documented honest-scope limitation). The same class of "measured-but-not-enforced / silent-pass" CI hole was already accepted and fixed in this repo (PASS-2.md documents the analogous pip-audit `--strict` fix), which supports treating this as an in-scope, fixable defect.

SEVERITY: medium is appropriate — it does not cause incorrect runtime behavior, but it silently weakens the test-quality gate the project claims to enforce. Fix is a one-line `--cov-fail-under=<N>` (or a `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under` in pyproject), choosing a baseline at or near current coverage.

Verified all three components of the claim empirically.

1. CI command (.github/workflows/ci.yml:105-106): the integration job runs exactly `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` with NO `--cov-fail-under` flag. Confirmed.

2. No coverage threshold anywhere: grep across pyproject.toml, .coveragerc, setup.cfg, tox.ini, pytest.ini found NO `[tool.coverage]` section, no `fail_under`, no `--cov-fail-under`. There is no .coveragerc file. The `[tool.pytest.ini_options]` block has no `addopts` injecting a coverage gate. Confirmed.

3. pyproject.toml:41 frames the dependency as `"pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs \`pytest --cov\` (#103)` — referencing issue #103 about gating coverage — yet nothing enforces a minimum. Confirmed.

4. Reproduced the behavioral defect: I created a throwaway module with one covered function and two large uncovered functions (43% coverage) and a single passing test, then ran `python -m pytest -q --cov=mymod --cov-report=term-missing`. Result: EXIT CODE 0, coverage reported 43% but the run passed. This proves that `pytest --cov` without a threshold exits 0 regardless of how low coverage falls, so a refactor deleting tests or adding a large untested module still passes CI green.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect: the CI coverage step is a vacuous gate. It measures and prints coverage but never fails on regression, providing a false sense of enforcement (especially given the comment implying it gates coverage per #103). It is in scope (CI/ops quality), not covered by any out-of-scope-by-design exclusion. Severity medium is appropriate — it does not break correctness but defeats the stated purpose of the coverage measurement and allows silent coverage erosion.
